symbols: cross — the instrument of salvation and the means of atonement
gold background — the divine, eternal realm in which the sacrifice occurs
Mary in blue — the Mother of Sorrows witnessing her Son's death
Mary Magdalene in red at the foot of the cross — passionate grief and devotion
John in pink-red — the beloved disciple faithfully present
haloes — the sanctity of all present at the cross
Christ's outstretched arms — embrace of all humanity in redemptive love
description: A Gothic panel painting showing Christ crucified on a wooden cross against a gold background. Christ wears a white loincloth and a halo, his body thin and pale with arms outstretched. At the top of the cross, a dark wreath or pelican motif is visible. Three haloed figures stand below: to the left, the Virgin Mary in a dark blue mantle with a grief-stricken posture; at the foot of the cross, Mary Magdalene in a bright orange-red robe kneels embracing the base; to the right, Saint John the Evangelist in a red-pink robe stands with hands clasped.
theological_description: The Crucifixion is the central event of Christian theology — the moment when God's justice and mercy converge in the sacrificial death of His Son. 'God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). The gold background transforms the historical execution into a cosmic, eternal event — this is not merely a death but the sacrifice 'once for all' (Hebrews 10:10) that reconciles God and humanity. Mary's presence fulfills Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce her soul (Luke 2:35), while John's faithfulness at the cross leads to Jesus's final act of filial love: 'Woman, here is your son... Here is your mother' (John 19:26-27). Mary Magdalene, embracing the foot of the cross, embodies the truth that those who have been forgiven much love much (Luke 7:47). Christ's outstretched arms on the cross paradoxically express the most expansive embrace in history — as He declared: 'And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself' (John 12:32). The crucifixion fulfills Isaiah's Suffering Servant prophecy: 'He was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him' (Isaiah 53:5).

scripture_refs.reason: Mary, John, and Mary Magdalene at the foot of the cross — the three figures directly depicted
He was pierced for our transgressions — the Suffering Servant prophecy fulfilled on the cross
God demonstrating His love through Christ dying for sinners — the theological core of the crucifixion
Sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all
Christ lifted up drawing all people to Himself — His arms outstretched on the cross
The sword piercing Mary's soul — fulfilled in her grief at the crucifixion
